preFix.title: Decorated paste-paper endpapers
preFix.subtitle: Wheat-paste patterns made directly on the endpaper sheet
preFix.excerpt: Paste paper is made by spreading tinted wheat-paste onto a pre-dampened sheet and dragging a comb or fingers through the wet paste. It is simpler chemistry than carrageenan marbling and produces a matte, painterly surface with an unmistakeable handmade quality. This tutorial makes paste paper directly on the endpaper sheet for pasting into a case-bound book.
preFix.sourceNotes: Wheat-starch paste as a bookbinding adhesive and paste-paper base is documented in Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), Internet Archive: chapter on adhesives and papers. The decorated paste-paper tradition in German and Dutch bookbinding from the 17th century onward is public domain; the technique has not changed in its essentials. Iron oxide pigments for colouring paste are documented as stable, lightfast earth pigments in general public-domain colour literature.

content.text: Paste paper

is the most accessible of the decorated paper traditions. The materials are inexpensive: plain flour, water, and a little acrylic or gouache pigment. Unlike carrageenan marbling, no special tray is needed, and there is no mordanting step. The paste spreads directly onto the
endpaper
sheet, and the pattern is made immediately in the wet paste. The tradition reached its height in German and Dutch
case binding
in the 17th century. Each session produces sheets that are slightly different from one another, which is part of what makes them useful as endpapers.

items.qty: 2 sheets per book, cut slightly oversize for trimming after drying
items.name: Endpaper stock (120–140 gsm cartridge or similar)
items.substitutions: Any smooth 120–140 gsm paper. Avoid heavily sized paper; it resists the paste.

items.name: Wheat-paste (cooked from plain flour)
items.substitutions: Methylcellulose paste also works; the working time is longer and the surface slightly different

items.qty: 1 per colour, plus 1 for the plain paste layer
items.name: Paste brush (50 mm flat)
Marbling comb
items.substitutions: A fork, a wide-toothed hair comb, or the tip of a bone folder all make drag patterns

items.name: PVA, acid-free (for pasting down into the case)
Clean water in a spray bottle or with a wide soft brush
Blotting paper or a clean flat board

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones: blunt blades skid. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing cutting mat. Always cut against a metal ruler. Bookbinding adhesives: PVA and wheat-paste are safe for indoor use without special ventilation. Clean brushes with warm water immediately after use. Never burn book glues; heat releases acrid fumes.

content.text: Cooking the wheat-paste

Mix 2 tablespoons of plain flour with enough cold water to make a smooth lump-free slurry: approximately 120 ml water, added gradually. Heat the mixture in a small saucepan over medium heat, stirring continuously. The paste will thicken as it approaches 80°C. Keep stirring until the paste is smooth, translucent, and thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. Remove from the heat and allow to cool to room temperature before using.

The paste should feel like a thick cream: it spreads when pushed with a brush but does not run off the paper. Too thin (porridge-water consistency) and the drag lines collapse; too thick (sticky dough consistency) and the comb drags rather than gliding. Adjust with a few drops of water or by reheating briefly to cook off excess water.
Tinting the paste

Divide the cooled paste into as many small dishes as you have colours. Stir a small amount of acrylic or gouache pigment into each: one teaspoon of pigment per cup of paste gives a medium tone. Acrylic pigments are water-clean-up; gouache gives a slightly chalky, powdery surface when dry. Both work well. Earth pigments (ochre, raw umber, iron oxide red) give the matte palette most associated with traditional paste papers.
Preparing the endpaper sheet
Lay the endpaper sheet on a flat, waterproof surface. Lightly mist the sheet with clean water, or brush a thin wash of plain water over the surface with a wide brush. This
size dampening

slows absorption: the wet surface keeps the paste workable for two to three minutes rather than beginning to dry at the edges almost immediately. The paper will cockle slightly when damp; this is expected.
Spreading and patterning

Spread the tinted paste quickly and evenly over the whole sheet with the flat paste brush. Use the brush to spread both horizontally and vertically so the paste covers without gaps. The layer should be thick enough to cover the paper surface fully but not so thick that it pools: roughly 1-2 mm.

Work the pattern immediately, before the paste begins to dry. Drag the marbling comb across the wet paste in parallel strokes from head to tail. The comb teeth lift channels of paste, revealing the lighter paper beneath. Then drag across the first lines from left to right: the result is a simple wavy-grid pattern. For a more complex pattern, drag the comb in a slight S-curve rather than a straight line.

Alternative patterns: finger-tips drawn through the wet paste leave wide channels; a crumpled piece of bubble wrap pressed into the surface and lifted leaves a grid of dots; a folded piece of card dragged at an angle creates fan shapes. There are no rules: the paste captures whatever mark is made in it.

If the paste starts to skin over before the pattern is complete, mist the surface lightly with water and continue. The window for reworking is shorter in warm weather and longer in cool, damp conditions.
Drying and pressing

Lift the sheet carefully and peg or lay it flat on a drying surface. The sheet will buckle as it dries. Leave it to dry completely: at least an hour in a warm room, longer in a damp environment. Do not force-dry with heat.

Once dry, press the sheet between blotting paper under a heavy flat weight overnight. The paste layer needs to be completely dry before pressing, or the pattern will transfer to the blotting paper. The pressed sheet emerges flatter but retains the slight surface relief of the paste pattern.
Trimming and pasting down

Trim the dried endpaper sheet to the correct size for the case. Score the fold line with the bone folder against the metal ruler before folding, because the paste layer is stiffer than uncoated paper and will crack at the fold without a clean score. Fold and press the crease firmly.

Paste the endpaper into the case using acid-free PVA diluted with a little water (a ratio of 4 parts PVA to 1 part water gives a workable viscosity). Brush the PVA onto the board face, not onto the paste paper. Brushing directly onto the paste paper can lift the paste surface. Press the endpaper down, smooth with the bone folder, and weight under a flat board overnight.
Variations

Two-colour paste paper: spread one colour, make a pattern, allow to dry partially, then spread a lighter or contrasting colour over the top and pattern again. The second layer partially fills the channels left by the first, giving a layered depth.

Paste paper made on plain cartridge paper makes good gift wrap or book cover paper. Endpaper stock is heavier because it has a structural role. For decorative sheets with no structural job, lighter paper is easier to work with.
